straight_beat.name: Harlan Forsythe
straight_beat.column:
The Giants lost again, and the standings are a cruel mirror. You don’t need a crystal ball to see the numbers: 6-11, seven games behind in the NL West. But let’s not waste time on the scoreboard. The real story is in the margins, the small cracks in the armor of a team that’s been told it’s close, over and over. Close enough to make you wonder if the front office has ever actually watched a game.  

Last night’s 2-1 loss to the Reds wasn’t just another setback. It was a textbook case of the Giants’ offense doing what it does best: showing up, then disappearing. They had their chances. They had their moments. But when the dust settled, they left the plate empty-handed. The Reds, for all their flaws, didn’t flinch. They bunted. They stole bases. They hit the ball where it could hurt. The Giants? They hit the ball where it couldn’t.  

And let’s talk about the pitching. Webb lasted seven innings, which is fine, but it’s not enough. The bullpen? That’s where the real trouble lies. You can’t rely on a reliever to get out of a jam if they’re throwing 98 mph and missing bats. The Giants’ relievers have been a mixed bag of fire and smoke, and the smoke is getting thicker. If you’re going to ask a pitcher to carry a game, you’d better make sure they’re carrying the right tools.  

The

optimist.name: Fiona Delgado-Chen
optimist.column:
The Giants lost again, but here’s the thing: they’re still in the race. Not because they’re winning, but because they’re learning. One-run losses build character. By June this club is playing the kind of late-inning baseball that buys you three rings in five years.  

Let’s not mince words. The 6-11 record feels like a punch to the gut, but I’m not here to dwell on the pain. I’m here to remind you that this team hasn’t given up. They’re still out there grinding, still out there trying to figure out how to win. The Reds didn’t just beat them—they beat them in a way that makes you want to throw your coffee cup at the wall. But that’s the thing about torture baseball, my favorite. It’s not about the immediate pain; it’s about the resilience you build when you keep showing up, even when the scoreboard isn’t smiling.  

Look, the Giants are in a rough patch, but they’re not without tools. Their bullpen, for all its flaws, has moments of brilliance. Their offense? It’s like a coffee roaster’s dream—slow, deliberate, and waiting for the right heat to unlock the flavor. The key is patience. You don’t wake up a sleeping giant with a single cup of espresso. You brew

pessimist.name: Bud Petrowski
pessimist.column:
The Giants lost again. Not that it’s news, but the way they lost—another walk-off, another collapse in the ninth, another reminder that this team isn’t just failing to win, it’s failing to *play* baseball. The Reds scored two runs in the bottom of the ninth, and somehow, the Giants didn’t have a single answer. Not a single one.  

Let’s start with the bullpen. Again. Because why not? The closer, the setup man, the long man—everyone’s got a name, but no identity. You’d think with the payroll they’re paying, they’d have a bullpen that *matters*. Instead, we get a parade of guys who look like they’re trying to impress their high school coach.
